perturbation.original_text: SELLER agrees to comply with all applicable Federal, State and local laws, executive orders, rules and regulations applicable to its performance under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: SELLER will endeavor to comply with applicable Federal, State and local laws, executive orders, rules and regulations applicable to its performance under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to comply' to 'will endeavor to comply' introduces ambiguity. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt rather than a guarantee of compliance, weakening the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FLSA mandates minimum wage requirements. By stating the seller will 'endeavor' to comply, there is no guarantee they will actually pay minimum wage, which is a violation of federal law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: in the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA). The federal minimum wage is $7.25 per hour effective July 24, 2009. Many states also have minimum wage laws. Some state laws provide greater employee protections; employers must comply with both. The FLSA does not provide wage payment collection procedures for an employee’s usual or promised wages or commissions in excess of those required by the FLSA. However, some states do have laws under which such claims (sometimes including fringe benefits) may be filed. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 29 - LABOR 29 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 29 - LABOR CHAPTER 8 - FAIR LABOR STANDARDS Sec. 206 - Minimum wage From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §206. Minimum wage (a) Employees engaged in commerce; home workers in Puerto Rico and Virgin Islands; employees in American Samoa; seamen on American vessels; agricultural employees Every employer shall pay to each of his employees who in any workweek is engaged in commerce or in the production of goods for commerce, or is employed in an enterprise engaged in commerce or in the production of goods for commerce, wages at the following rates: (1) except as otherwise provided in this section, not less than— (A) $5.85 an hour, beginning on the 60th day after May 25, 2007; (B) $6.55 an hour, beginning 12 months after that 60th day; and (C) $7.25 an hour, beginning 24 months after that 60th day; (2) if such employee is a home worker in Puerto Rico or the Virgin Islands, not less than the minimum piece rate prescribed by regulation or order; or, if no such minimum piece rate is in effect, any piece rate adopted by such employer which shall yield, to the proportion or class of employees prescribed by regulation or order, not less than the applicable minimum hourly wage rate. Such minimum piece rates or employer piece rates shall be commensurate with, and shall be paid in lieu of, the minimum hourly wage rate applicable under the provisions of this section. The Administrator, or his authorized representative, shall have power to make such regulations or orders as are necessary or appropriate to carry out any of the provisions of this paragraph, including the power without limiting the generality of the foregoing, to define any operation or occupation which is performed by such home work employees in Puerto Rico or the Virgin Islands; to establish minimum piece rates for any operation or occupation so defined; to prescribe the method and procedure for ascertaining and promulgating minimum piece rates; to prescribe standards for employer piece rates, including the proportion or class of employees who shall
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "agrees to comply" with "will endeavor to comply," which weakens the obligation to follow applicable laws. The law snippets, particularly U.S.C. Title 29, Section 206, mandate that every employer 'shall pay' minimum wage. The original text ensures compliance, but the changed text introduces ambiguity, suggesting the seller might not fully comply with wage laws, potentially violating the FLSA.

perturbation.original_text: Written approval shall not be unreasonably withheld by BUYER and shall be in accordance with the requirements of the Prime Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: Written approval shall not be unreasonably withheld by BUYER and should generally be in accordance with the requirements of the Prime Contract.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be' to 'should generally be' introduces ambiguity regarding the BUYER's obligation to adhere to the Prime Contract requirements. The term 'generally' gives the BUYER leeway to deviate from the Prime Contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Work Hours and Safety Standards Act (CWHSSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: CWHSSA requires contractors to comply with overtime pay requirements. If the Prime Contract mandates specific overtime provisions due to CWHSSA, allowing the BUYER to only 'generally' adhere to those requirements creates a potential violation, as the subcontractor could be denied proper overtime compensation based on the 'general' rather than strict adherence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall be" to "should generally be" weakens the BUYER's obligation to adhere to the Prime Contract requirements. This introduces ambiguity and allows for deviation, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation suggests that if the Prime Contract mandates specific overtime provisions due to CWHSSA, the modified text creates a potential violation. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the potential for non-compliance with overtime pay requirements creates a contradiction. The original text mandates adherence, whereas the changed text creates a loophole, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All representations and certifications which have been submitted to the BUYER in connection with the award of this Agreement are incorporated herein and made a part hereof and such have been relied upon by the BUYER in issuing this Agreement. SELLER agrees to promptly advise the BUYER should there be any change in status with respect to the matters covered by such representations and certifications.
perturbation.changed_text: All representations and certifications which have been submitted to the BUYER in connection with the award of this Agreement are incorporated herein and made a part hereof and such have been relied upon by the BUYER in issuing this Agreement. SELLER agrees to attempt to advise the BUYER should there be any change in status with respect to the matters covered by such representations and certifications.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees to promptly advise' with 'agrees to attempt to advise' weakens the SELLER's obligation to keep the BUYER informed of changes in status. 'Attempt' suggests that the SELLER may not actually fulfill the advisory role, leading to a lack of transparency.
perturbation.law_explanation: FAR 52.209-5 requires offerors to certify regarding debarment, suspension, proposed debarment, and other responsibility matters. By changing the obligation to 'attempt to advise,' the SELLER might not promptly inform the BUYER of a debarment, suspension, or proposed debarment, thus preventing the BUYER from fulfilling its obligation to avoid contracting with a suspended or debarred entity. This failure to disclose relevant information about the seller's responsibility status directly contradicts FAR requirements.

(1) Federal taxes are considered delinquent if both of the following criteria apply: (i) The tax liability is finally de
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'agrees to promptly advise' to 'agrees to attempt to advise' introduces ambiguity that weakens the seller's obligation to inform the buyer of changes in their responsibility status, as it relates to debarment, suspension, or proposed debarment. FAR 52.209-5, as seen in both scraped snippets, requires offerors to certify regarding responsibility matters. The change could allow the seller to claim they 'attempted' to advise even if they did not adequately inform the buyer, thereby undermining the buyer's ability to comply with regulations preventing contracts with debarred or suspended entities. This weakens a core component of ensuring responsible contracting and contradicts the intent of FAR 52.209-5 by creating a loophole that could prevent the buyer from receiving critical information.
